<br>
 
### **Elastic net penalised regression with nested cross-validation** 
[Go back to index](#top)

#### **Model justification**
[Go back to index](#top)

Our objective was to evaluate the predictive power of maternal/child immune markers and child neurometabolites (separately, jointly, and with effect modification by maternal HIV) for child **ELOM language scores**. Based on upstream analyses, we restricted the immune panel to markers found to be associated with language outcomes, and neurometabolites which had shown consistent group differences and/or association with language outcomes too.

Two considerations guided the modelling strategy:

- Since we were creating many interaction terms between variables, we knew that some predictors would be strongly correlated with each other (**multicollinearity**). We also knew that the total number of predictors would become much larger than the number of participants in the study (**p≫n**). This combination made standard regression unreliable unless we used methods that could handle many correlated variables.

- We wanted to measure how well the model predicts **new**, **unseen data**, not just the data we trained it on. We also wanted to find out which predictors were consistently important, even when we repeated the analysis multiple times and filled in missing values in different plausible ways.

Accordingly, we used **elastic net regression within nested cross-validation**.

To handle missingness, we used **multiple imputation by chained equations** (`mice`) with 5 imputations, stratified by `maternal_hiv`. Stratification preserved distinct marginal distributions and cross-variable relationships within HEU vs HU groups, in case missingness or variable distributions differed by maternal HIV. Whenever we imputed missing values, we used only the training data for that round of cross-validation. The models we used to impute missing values were never allowed to see the test data. This prevented “information leakage”, which would make performance look better than it really is. Then, the model re-calculated all the interaction variables from scratch, using the imputed main variables, so that they matched perfectly.

Elastic net required predictors to be on comparable scales, to balance the penalty across coefficients. Although immune markers were already log-transformed and neurometabolites had been scaled upstream, we standardized all predictors inside each training fold before model fitting. This yields standardized coefficients (change in ELOM per 1 SD change in predictor, conditional on the rest) and ensures the penalty does not privilege variables with larger raw scales. **The outcome was not standardized so that RMSE remains in ELOM scores**.

<br>

#### **Model training**
[Go back to index](#top)

We tested our model using two levels of cross-validation:

- **Outer cross-validation** (5 folds): This step estimated how well the model would work on entirely new data. We split the dataset into 5 parts, trained the model on 4 parts, and tested on the remaining part, repeating so that every part served as a test set once. We also kept the proportions of children with and without maternal HIV the same in each fold.

- **Inner cross-validation** (5 folds): Inside each outer training set, we did another split to figure out the best model settings (alpha parameter). This tuning process was repeated for each of the 5 versions of the data where missing values were filled in.

For each outer test set:

- We re-created all the necessary interaction variables from the raw test data

- If any predictor values were still missing, we filled them in using the average values from the training data (not the test data)

- We scaled the test data so it used the same mean and standard deviation as the training data

- We made predictions using each of the 5 imputation-specific models and averaged them

We measured prediction error (RMSE) and how much of the variation in the outcome was explained (R²) for each outer fold. Then we averaged these across folds. We also calculated RMSE separately for HEU and HU groups to see if the model worked better in one group than the other.

Instead of relying on one “final” model, we looked at results from all 25 models (5 folds × 5 imputations). For each variable we calculated:

- The average effect size (coefficient)

- How often it was included in the model (non-zero coefficient)

- How frequently it was selected across all models

This approach shows which predictors are truly stable and consistently important, rather than those that appear only in one lucky run of the model.

<br>

##### **Model choice and R code**
[Go back to index](#top)

We explored whether the inclusion or exclusion of certain neurometabolites in the elastic net model would modify predictive performance and alter variable stability patterns. Three models were compared:

- Model 1: Left parietal white matter choline ratios. RMSE=4.45 ±1.10, R²=0.128 ±0.093, empirical p≈0.02, with choline ratios selected in 3-way interactions in ~70% of models. In upstream analyses, left parietal white matter choline ratios to creatine were lower in children who are HEU. They were also found to be associated with ELOM language and literacy scores in HU children.

- Model 2: Left parietal white matter choline ratios, and midline parietal grey matter glutamate ratios. RMSE=4.36 ±1.03, R²=0.107 ±0.060, empirical p≈0.02, with glutamate and choline selected in 3-way interactions in ~60% and ~52% of models, respectively. In upstream analyses, midline parietal grey matter glutamate ratios to creatine were lower in children who are HEU. They were also found to be associated with a number of maternal and child immune markers.

- Model 3: Left parietal white matter choline ratios, and midline parietal grey matter glutamate and myo-inositol ratios. RMSE=4.39 ±0.93, R²=0.110 ±0.074, empirical p≈0.01, with choline, glutamate, and myo-inositol ratios selected in 3-way interactions in ~70%, ~60%, and ~30% of models, respectively. In upstream analyses, midline parietal grey matter myo-inositol ratios to creatine were found to be associated with a number of maternal and child immune markers. They were also found to be associated with ELOM language and literacy scores in crude analyses, but significance was lost after multiple comparison correction.

Although predictive performance differences between models were small, Model 3 showed the lowest RMSE and lowest permutation p-value, while retaining biologically plausible neurometabolites with complementary mechanistic relevance. Given these features, Model 3 was selected for subsequent interpretation.

<br>

###### **Model performance**

THe overall predictive accuracy for our model was **RMSE=4.39 ±0.93 ELOM points**; **R²=0.11 ±0.08** across outer folds.

The model explains ~11% of variation in language scores. For complex neurodevelopmental outcomes driven by multiple biological and psychosocial factors, this can be considered modest but meaningful. 

Performance was similar across groups (HU RMSE=4.57, HEU RMSE=4.10), with slightly better accuracy in the HEU group, suggesting the feature patterns (especially interactions) may capture more systematic variance among children who are HEU.

<br>

###### **Permutation test**

Does observed predictive signal exceed chance? 

The predictive performance of the elastic net model (mean R²=0.11) exceeded chance levels (empirical p≈0.01) based on 100 label-permutation tests, indicating that the selected maternal immune, neurometabolite, and HIV-interaction features contain non-random information about child ELOM language scores. However, the proportion of variance explained remained modest.

<br>

###### **Top predictors**

When looking at variables selected in more than 50% of all elastic net models, most of the strongest predictors of ELOM language scores were immune markers, many of them measured in early- or mid-childhood. The **top predictor** was **neuroinflammatory marker YKL-40 at 6 weeks of age**, selected in all models. It had a negative coefficient, indicating that higher concentrations were associated with lower language scores. 

Other immune markers (child IL-7 at 2 years, maternal IL-8, child IL-5 at 3 years, child IL-13 at 2 and 3 years, and IFNγ at 2 years) also showed negative associations, while others (child YKL-40 at 2 years, maternal IL-13, infant TNFα, child IL-10 at 5 years) showed positive associations.

Regarding **maternal HIV-modified effects**, the **top predictor** was a three-way association with a negative effect on ELOM language scores in 72% of elastic net models:

- child pro-inflammatory cytokine IL-6 at age 2 years;

- left parietal white matter choline ratios to creatine at age 6-7 years; and

- maternal HIV status

This interaction reflected a biologically layered interaction, where child inflammation, white matter maturation, and HIV exposure combined to influence language outcomes.

Below it, other frequent HIV-modified predictors were:

- In 60% of models, a three-way interaction (Child IL-6 at age 2 years × parietal grey matter glutamate ratios to creatine × maternal HIV) with a positive effect on ELOM language scores

- In 56% of models, a two-way interaction (maternal IL-13 × HIV) with a positive effect on ELOM language scores

- In 52% of models, a single association of maternal HIV with poorer ELOM language scores

Apart from these, no other frequent predictors (present in >50% of models) showed interactions with maternal HIV, meaning that while some immune markers were strongly predictive of language scores, their associations were generally similar across HEU and HU groups.

Overall, the aim was to explore whether immune markers or neurometabolite levels could be used to predict language scores, and in particular, whether predictors capturing group differences in language performance (through interactions with maternal HIV) could enhance predictive performance. The stability results suggest that while certain interaction terms with maternal HIV were indeed selected, they were less frequent than the main immune markers, except for the child IL6 × choline ratios × HIV interaction.

<br>

###### **Predictors that showed interaction with maternal HIV status**

A total of 59 predictors involved an interaction with maternal HIV, most of which were three-way combinations with immune markers and neurometabolite ratios. The most consistently selected HIV-related term was the three-way interaction between year 2 IL-6, parietal white matter choline ratios, and maternal HIV (72% selection frequency; negative coefficient), indicating that the association between child IL-6 and mid-childhood language scores differed jointly by choline levels and HIV exposure. The next most stable was a similar three-way term involving year 2 IL-6, parietal grey matter glutamate ratios, and maternal HIV (60%; positive coefficient), suggesting a potential modulatory role of glutamate in this association. The top-ranked two-way interaction was maternal IL-13 × HIV (56%; positive coefficient), pointing to a stronger IL-13–language association in HEU as compared to HU children.

The first interaction involving myo-inositol appeared at 32% frequency: year 2 IL-5 × parietal white matter myo-inositol ratios × maternal HIV (negative coefficient). Several additional myo-inositol–based three-way terms appeared at lower frequencies (16–28%), often in combination with IL-13, IL-6, IL-5, IL-10, TNF-α, or YKL-40, and generally carried negative coefficients.

Overall, three-way interactions remained more prevalent than two-way ones among HIV-related predictors, particularly those combining immune markers with neurometabolites that have plausible biological roles: choline (white matter maturation and cell membrane turnover), glutamate (neurotransmission), and myo-inositol (neuroinflammation). While choline and glutamate terms were among the most stable interactions, myo-inositol–based terms were less consistently selected but still recurred across re-samples, indicating they may capture subtler HIV-related modulation of immune–brain relationships.
